chunks.text: NATO UNCLASSIFIED The Republic of EBEN Country Model (CM) Guidance and caveats: The EBEN Country Model (CM) is a Joint Warfare Centre product issued solely to support NATO exercises. No part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This document is a NATO Unclassified document that conta ins a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources and adapted to the NATO exercises. Although the CM draws on real world events and data, the actors and dynamics portrayed have been adapted in order to m eet NATO Training Audience (TA) needs. For this reason, some actors are given a more prominent role. Furthermore, images, maps, names and other supporting information such as that used for biographies do not always describe a real character, location, or s ocial characteristic (historical/religious/political etc.). This to ensure , inter alia, that the CM adheres to national caveats and sensitivities. Any remaining identified sensitivities should be communicated to JWC so that these can be addressed. User Gu ide: This setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional details to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the CM is designed t o avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the TA. Geostrategic and regional context Historical timeline BEFORE 1899 1899 - 1960 1960 - 1972 1972 - 1990 1990 - PRESENT The kingdom of EBEN began to develop around 900 CE. After the rise, the golden age (1300 -1700 CE), the international trade period, the fall of one of the most significant and long - lasting kingdoms in West African history is dated to 1897, after decades of civil wars and power struggles. 1485 : First Portuguese ships arrived and Portuguese involvement. 1553 : First British ships arrived and involvement. 1704 : First port build by France. 1889 : Last independent ruler was defeated. 1899 -1960: The Colonial period of EBEN. The country gradually was granted more autonomy, especially after WW -II. 1958 : Became an autonomous republic. 1960 : Gained full independence . This marked the end of the colonial period and the beginning of the era as a sovereign nation. A succession of military coups brought many changes of government. The last of these brought a regime professing strict Marxist -Leninist principles. The 1972 coup : Military officers of northern ethnic background ended the rotational presidential system. last of these brought a regime professing strict Marxist -Leninist principles. The 1972 coup : Military officers of northern ethnic background ended the rotational presidential system. The country applied a Marxist -Leninist policy . In 1975 the name EBEN was introduced (the People's Republic of EBEN). The People's Revolutionary Party remained in complete power until 1990. 1989 : The abolition of Marxism -Leninism. 1989 -90: A n ew Constitution establish ed with a multiparty system and democracy. EBEN was thus the first African country to successfully complete the transition from a dictatorship to a pluralistic political system. 1990: Beginning of a politically relatively stable period with democratic governance being gradually established. All major ethnic groups are represented in a power sharing agreement. 2002: First municipal elections since before the institution of Marxism - Leninism. Since the late 2010s: The majority factions have been increasingly assertive in the political arena, excluding political opposition from elections. This has at times sparked viole nt protest across the country. Current : EBEN is currently facing increasing challenges. The first two decade s following EBEN independence in 1960 , saw civil rule interrupted by successive coups, military rule, and then the Marxist -Leninist period between 1972 and 1989. Since 1990 has EBEN been relatively stable, experiencing democratization and the introduction of a multiparty political system where power is shared between the four most significant ethnic groups. However, recent restricting of political opposition has at times sparked violent protests. Climate changes and rapidly growing population is adding to polit ical and socio -economic challenges . NATO UNCLASSIFIED Geostrategic interests and foreign policy EBEN is one of the better example s of democratic progress and stability in sub -Saharan West Africa since the 1990s . EBEN has focused on sustain ing internal stability , thus it does also need to do more to address threats with a regional dimension such as that of Terror Groups (TG s) and Violent Extremist Organisations (VEO s). Along with other armed groups, TGs and VEOs continue to exploit ungoverned spaces in the region and are gradua lly penetrating southwards and expanding from the SAHEL into the southern states along the Gulf of GUINEA (GoG) , and further towards western states of Senegal, Guinea and especially CERO. EBEN has had to respond to such threats without disrupting its inter nal political and socio -cultural equilibrium, whilst also facing the additional challenges brought about by globalization, climate change, demographics migration and an evolving international system. The strategic outlook demonstrates EBEN's commitment t o comprehensive economic and social development, with a focus on industrialization, infrastructure improvement, fiscal responsibility, and adaptation to climate change challenges. The strategic and foreign policy objectives of EBEN include:  Economic Growth and Development by achieving a strong, inclusive, sustained, and durable economic growth , focusing on economic diversification and modernization of key sectors as agriculture, transportation, infrastructure, and services (logistics, digital, tourism, and financial).  Industrial Development by encouraging local processing of agriculture products (as cashew nuts), and by bolstering manufacturing output and exports.  Private Sector Development by prioritizing private sector partnerships and financing for climat e investments and overall economic development.  Fiscal consolidation by pursuing update of national fiscal policy, aiming to increase tax revenues and rationalize public expenditure.  Infrastructure upgrade by improving the competit iveness of the Port of

prioritizing private sector partnerships and financing for climat e investments and overall economic development.  Fiscal consolidation by pursuing update of national fiscal policy, aiming to increase tax revenues and rationalize public expenditure.  Infrastructure upgrade by improving the competit iveness of the Port of Co tonou , including extension of the port basin and build ing a new terminal.  Regional Integration and Security by leveraging its strategic position for transit trade and regional integration. The country is addressing security challenges, particularly in the north, through military expansion and international partnerships.  Strengthen diplomatic relations to enhance regional and international cooperation and to address regional cross -border security issues and regional economic collaboration and integration.  Enhance Climate Change Adaptation by adapting agricultural practices, restoring and protecting forests, investing in water resources, and strengthening resilience to urban flooding. Adapting to climate change is seen as crucial for sustainable and resilien t economic growth. NATO UNCLASSIFIED 2. Socio -economic and physical environment NATO UNCLASSIFIED Overview of society Population Over 14 million. Official language French. There are approximate ly 20 national languages of which Tehoue, Bayougi and Owochi -Buruba are the three most commonly spoken. Currency West African Communauté Financière Africaine (CFA) Franc (XOF) (1 XOF = 0,0016 USD). Demographics Median age: 17.5 years. Life expectancy at birth: 60 years. Approximately 50 % of the population is under 18 years and only 10 .5% of the population is over 50 years. Estimated annual population growth rate is approximately 2.7 % . An estimated 350,000 youth reach military -eligible age a nnually. People and ethnicity EBEN has a complex ethnic landscape with more than 60 different ethnic groups. military -eligible age a nnually. People and ethnicity EBEN has a complex ethnic landscape with more than 60 different ethnic groups. (see map 1 , for the most significant ) Religion 52% Christians , 25%. Muslims , 18% traditional indigenous religions, and 5% with no religious affiliation . The secular constitution provides for freedom of religious thought, expression, and practice. Family and gender EBEN is a predominantly patriarchal society. While EBEN is a signatory to the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW), women are prevented from reaching their full potential because of discriminatory social norms, incentives and legal institutions. In most ethnic groups, however, mothers and sisters have influence in family life. Education The literacy rate is around 38.4% (males 49.9%, females: 27.3%). EBEN has achieved near universal primary education, while ar ound 54% of children are enrolled in secondary education. Tertiary level enrolment is around 12% of the 18 -25-year age cohort. State primary and secondary level education is free. Culture and arts In line with its ethnic diversity and century -old traditio ns, EBEN has wide -ranging cultures and traditions. These are however predominantly clustered in two distinct regions, the largely Muslim north and the largely animist and Christian south. French influence has been most significant among the educated segmen ts and the elite of EBEN. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Ethnicity The Tehoue and the Ifu, two closely related Guinean ethnic groups, make up the largest ethnic groups in the southern part of EBEN, accounting for respectively around 36% and 11% of the population of EBEN. The Owochi and Baruba concentrated in the northern part of the country account for a further a 20% of the EBEN population. The Bayougi, comprising about 9% of the population and with a large community in neighbouring IGAINER,

and 11% of the population of EBEN. The Owochi and Baruba concentrated in the northern part of the country account for a further a 20% of the EBEN population. The Bayougi, comprising about 9% of the population and with a large community in neighbouring IGAINER, dominate the southeast (see m ap below). NATO UNCLASSIFIED Economy EBEN’s economy is reliant on agricultural production - particularly cotton - as well as formal and informal re -export and transit trade with IGAINER. The country’s geographical position at the juncture of two major regional corridors, the IDAVILLE -Lagos and Cotonou -Gouncoro corridors, makes this West African country an important commercial and tourism hub. Officially, EBEN has a mixed economic system , however, subsistence farming and its underdeveloped industries are more akin to those of a traditional lower income economy. The country’s primary source of revenue is the port of Cotonou, which accounts for as much as 40% of EBEN’s national budget. EBEN leverages its port as a platform to reach over 100 million consumers in neighbouring landlocked countries such as FANBO, MILA, REGIN, and CHAD, and 155 million in IGAINER. A large informal sector, estimated at over 48% of GDP, is based primarily on unregu lated trade with IGAINER. Sharing a long border with its neighbouring economic powerhouse, EBEN’s economy is highly dependent on serving the IGAINER market, almost exclusively informally. High levels of t rade protection in IGAINER have created profitable o pportunities for EBEN’s informal traders who import goods for re -export to IGAINER across a porous border. EBEN is also positioned as a transit corridor for the landlocked countries to its north . It represents the shortest and most competitive transport ro ute to Gouncoro and is a competitive option for goods destined for OWOTSHA , northern IGAINER and parts of

border. EBEN is also positioned as a transit corridor for the landlocked countries to its north . It represents the shortest and most competitive transport ro ute to Gouncoro and is a competitive option for goods destined for OWOTSHA , northern IGAINER and parts of MILA and CHAD. The transport industry and the port of Cotonou, referred to as the ‘lungs of t he economy’, play a primary role in EBEN’s economy. Gove rnment spending amount to around 23% of the country’s output (GDP). Public debt is equivalent to 54.6% of GDP, which is acceptable for a developing country, but masks EBEN’s dependency on trade taxes. In close collaboration with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank (WB), the government of EBEN has committed to structurally transform the country’s economy by implementing a rigid public expenditure policy. Ongo ing reforms have created fiscal space by stimulating growth in domestic revenue. Despite several efforts to improve internal tax collection, the predominance of the informal sector poses a challenge to longer -term economic growth. Overall, the main economic vulnerabilities in EBEN consist of global cotton and import prices and climat e change. Furthermore, the worsening security situation in EBEN’s northern areas could further compromise its economic outlook. EBEN has experienced sustained economic growth over the last few years, ranking for the first time as a lower middle -income econ omy. However, the country is still a long way from achieving sustainable poverty reduction . Social protection programs - essential to strengthening social inclusion, such as health and retirement insurance - still need to be implemented. Development is not geographically uniform, with urban and coastal areas benefiting significantly more than rural areas. Rising inequality has encouraged internal migration and deepened dissatisfaction among underprivileged people and unemployed young university graduates. NATO UNCLASSIFIED

NATO UNCLASSIFIED Economic sectors Port activity and the cotton sector are the largest drivers of economic growth in EBEN. President NEEBOBY’s rule is problematic given his conflict of interests as a major businessperson involved in the strategic sectors of the economy. NEEBOBY and his family have established complete control of the cotton sector, owning 51% of the Society for the Development of Cotton (SODECO) through his private companies and controlling the remaining 49% of shares held by the state as President. He als o has business interests relating to the port of Cotonou . The country’s GDP composition by sector consists of 51% services, 26% agriculture, and 23% manufacturing. The primary sector plays an important role in EBEN economy, generating 70% of employment. Since the mid -1980s, EBEN has produced yams, cassava, corn, millet, and beans to achieve self -sufficiency in staple foods. Among cash crops, the formerly predominant palm product output declined considerably in the 1980 s even though cotton output rose. The output of karit e, peanuts, cacao beans, and coffee also has increased. Livestock include cattle, goats, pigs, horses, and poultry. Cotton is the primary export commodity providing nearly 40% of EBEN ’s GDP and roughly 80% of official export receipts .1 The uptrend in cotton and food production has not however translated into better yields and an increase in per capita income because it stems primarily from an increas e in land area under cultivation. has not however translated into better yields and an increase in per capita income because it stems primarily from an increas e in land area under cultivation. Steady output growth has been accompanied by a deforestatio n rate that is more than twice the Sub -Saharan average. Sustainability issues arise in both agriculture and forestry due to trends in resource degradation, unclear tenure rights, and water stresses exacerbated by climate change. Substantial quantities of fish are caught annually in lagoons and rivers, while coastal fishing produces a smaller, but growing, amount of catch. Most of the fish is exported to IGAINER and GOOT. A modest fishing fleet provides fish and shrimp for local subsistence and export to Eu rope. Formerly government -owned commercial activities are now privatized , which makes the private commercial and agricultural sectors the principal contributors to growth in the primary sector. The manufacturing sector is confined to some light industry, m ainly involved in processing primary products and the cattle production of consumer goods. It has, however, been improving slightly since 2012, propelled by construction (itself driven by the rehabilitation of various roads and the construction of hotels i n Cotonou). In addition, as a leading cotton producer, EBEN is moving into processing finished goods (from the cotton industry) as a rout e to prosperity. The country’s agricultural centre, which used to produce high volumes of agricultural commodities (wi th most of the crops used to be sold raw without any transformation) , is now considering processing those items on its own national soil, instead of selling them raw. An initiative between EBEN and DUSHMAN, known as Integrated Industrial Platforms , is unde r way to create jobs and revenue. own national soil, instead of selling them raw. An initiative between EBEN and DUSHMAN, known as Integrated Industrial Platforms , is unde r way to create jobs and revenue. It aims to process cotton (as well as other agricultural products) locally, with the goal o f exporting finished goods, such as T-shirts and underwear, to Europe, Asia, Africa and the US A2. Lastly, the service sector has gr own quickly, stimulated by economic liberalization and fiscal reform, but also the use of modern technology such as automobil es and computers. 1 In the past few years, EBEN has become Africa’s leading cotton producer . 2 The African Union has selected the textile industry as a priority because it has been, and still is in many countries, the starting point for industrialization, therefore a sector in which job creation is encouraged. Moreover, the country’s position on the west coast of Africa is a logistical advantage for buyers in the US, Europe and Africa, as products will not have to be shipped so far. The initiative expects to increase GDP to USD 7 billion by 2030 and that EBEN manufacturing output fivefold. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Trade Foreign trade represents 63% of EBEN’s GDP - relatively high by regional comparison . The country is a member of various trade organizations including the World Trade Organization (WTO) and the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). The vast majority of its trade passes through the port of Cotonou, the capacity of which the go vernment wants to develop. While EBEN’s domestic market remains small, the port of Cotonou allows it to re -export goods to landlocked neighbours and IGAINER (to which it also serves as secondary port, thus holding a potential to earn lucrative customs duti es) reaching markets with an estimated 255 million consumers.

domestic market remains small, the port of Cotonou allows it to re -export goods to landlocked neighbours and IGAINER (to which it also serves as secondary port, thus holding a potential to earn lucrative customs duti es) reaching markets with an estimated 255 million consumers. The long border between EBEN with IGAINER, the economic powerhouse of West Africa, heavily influences the economic and social landscape of the country. Economic and social developments in IGAINE R exert a decisive influence on formal and informal sectors of EBEN, with traders rapidly exploiting differences in trade barriers and regulatio ns between IGAINER and EBEN, and fluctuations in the IGAINER Naira against the West African CFA Franc. Immense t ransit and informal trade flow at the Dantokpa market in Cotonou generate enormous economic benefits. With hundreds of thousa nds of daily clients, 20,000 vendors renting permanent shops and 25,000 itinerant vendors, Dantokpa market is the largest workplace in EBEN. Beyond its economic role, Dantokpa is a social and cultural hub , and it has also become an important site of political competition between national, regional and local networks. Overall, EBEN’s strategic geographical location, relatively calm soc ial climate and political stability are conducive to export growth geared towards IGAINER, as well as regional and global markets. However, the business climate needs to improve through, inter alia, more effective trade facilitation and tra de support institutions, better governance, and better delivery of public services. Private sector and business climate Not all major economic players in EBEN agree on the goal of building a liberal market economic system. Many political actors hesitate to support privatization and a substantial reform of the civil service, adhering instead to the idea that crucial industries should be in the hands of the state. Some entrepreneurs are not eager to create

of building a liberal market economic system. Many political actors hesitate to support privatization and a substantial reform of the civil service, adhering instead to the idea that crucial industries should be in the hands of the state. Some entrepreneurs are not eager to create more competition, as they enjoy the benefits of near monopolie s or oligopolies . Moreover, a segment of the population relies on the income from illegal smuggling activities. Overall, private companies are formally free to operate, although facing a number of institutional diff iculties. In addition to the privatizati on of the ports and the cotton sector, several other state companies and public services have seen their management entrusted to private parties (e.g., airports, school canteens, health insurance). EBEN continues efforts to attract private investment in su pport of economic growth despite reports of high-level corruption. There is an evident conflict of interest between the President’s role and his business interests. His priva te companies have regained control of key sectors of the economy, including the po rt and market of Cotonou, and the cotton sector. Considering that cotton and the import and re -export commodities to neighbouring countries are the main drivers of the EBEN economy, these sectors yield significant socio -political power and influence. In order to implement its development strategy and avoid dependency on international donors, the government has announced plans t o mobilise resources from the private sector. The World Bank judges that EBEN has improved the reliability and transparency of its land administration as well as the speed of procedures related to the transfer of property. However, EBEN’s business environment remains poor and still faces structural problems, s uch as access to credit, corruption and unfair tax rates. Despite the progre ss made, poor energy and transport infrastructure and a dominant informal sector constitute major challenges. NATO UNCLASSIFIED OVERVIEW: The economy of EBEN is dependent on the export of unprocessed agricultural products (primarily cotton and cashew nuts) and the re -export of imported goods and commodities (e.g., second -hand cars and rice) to IGAINER. EBEN ’s economy is ranked number 144 globally in terms of total exports, number 142 in total imports, and number 89 in terms of economic complex ity. EBEN major industries: Textiles, agro -processing industry, light engineering, telecommunication, cement production. EBEN major exports: gold ($849M), raw cotton ($625 M), nuts and cashews ($275M), other oily seeds ($43.6M), refined petroleum ($39.8M), exporting mostly to UNITED ARAB EMIRATES ($854M), BANGLADESH ($427M), DRENUR ($341M), and DUSHMAN ($128M). EBEN major imports: rice ($690M), cars ($343M), palm oil ($249M) and light pure woven cotton ($121M), importing mostly from DUSHMAN ($1.06B), DRENUR ($603M), USA ($317M), and FRANCE ($287M). Gross National Income explained : Because development encompasses many factors - economic, environmental, cultural, educational, and institutional - no single measure gives a complete picture. However, the total earnings of the residents of an economy, measured by its Gross National Income (GNI), is a good measure of its capacity to pr ovide for the well -being of its people. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Financial sector The financial sector in EBEN has developed considerably from a very low starting point. A series of reforms undertaken in the 1990s resulted in the consolidation of the banking sector and the privat ization of all state banks. However, the sector, aligned (in principle) with international standards,

sector in EBEN has developed considerably from a very low starting point. A series of reforms undertaken in the 1990s resulted in the consolidation of the banking sector and the privat ization of all state banks. However, the sector, aligned (in principle) with international standards, suffers from institutio nal weaknesses. Due to a large informal economy and widespread poverty, relatively few enterprises have access to the banking sys tem. The predominance of the informal and subsistence economies has encouraged the diffusion of microfinance institutions (MFIs). However, most of EBEN’s MFIs are not legally authorized, and the ir existence increases the risk of instability. EBEN is a membe r of the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU) , as such, the country cannot pursue an independent currency and foreign exchange rate policy. The currency is pegged to the euro, therefore heavily influenced by the European Central Bank’s anti -inflation policy. Economic foreign relations and international cooperation EBEN has a liberal institutional trade framework, but commercial exchanges are hampered by a lack of modern technical equipme nt and bureaucratic barriers. The country is a signatory to all the relevant international trade conventions of the World Trade Organization and the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development. EBEN is part of the trade agreements between the European Union, African Union, Caribbean and Pacifi c (ACP) countries, and a member of regional and sub -regional economic unions such as ECOWAS and WAEMU .3 Trade liberalization in EBEN has increased due to progress toward the adoption of an Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA) with the European Union, EBEN’s largest trading partner, and the launch of the Economic Community of the West African States (ECOWAS) Common External Tariff (CET). However, given its dependence on foreign assistance as a developing country without substantial natural resources, EB EN

However, given its dependence on foreign assistance as a developing country without substantial natural resources, EB EN has very little negotiation power vis -àvis international players over accepting or refusing international aid. Development partners have faith in EBEN’s attempts to advance democratic governance but are increasingly worried by the prevalence of corrupt ion and mismanagement in the state administration. This means that, although the government has nominally relative discretion on the allocation of aid, international partners are able to strongly influence the country’s policies an d priorities. Property r ights Property rights are formally defined in EBEN but cannot be considered fully safeguarded because of shortcomings in the rule o f law, particularly in terms of corruption. Most land in EBEN is customary land and the occupants lack formal documents to pr ove their ownership. The conventional process for land registration is too expensive and complex for most rural landholders. Moreover, customary practice excludes or limits access to land owner ship to vulnerable groups (women, migrants, pastoralists). 3 West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). EBEN has been a strong supporter of the regional WAEMU convergence schemes a nd fulfils the criteria of the West African Economic Union . Effective structural reforms in terms of investment and fiscal consolidation . Political stability. Weaknesses: Poorly diversified economy (exports based on cotton, cashew nuts, and re -exports) . Governance deficiencies: corruption, bureaucracy, political and judicial arbitrariness . Insecurity in the North due to ethnic and religious tensions. Low quality of infrastructure and problems related to electricity supply. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Socio -economic challenges  Governance: The state’s basic administrative structures extend throughout the country’s territory, but are centrally controlled. In rural areas, however, the performance of political and judicial decision -making is substandard, and the allocation of public goods is i nefficient. The largely donor -driven decentralization process has slowly started to improve socioeconomic conditions and strengthen the presence of basic administrative infrastructure. St ill, legal standards are not enforced, and corruption remains a syste mic problem.  Poverty: A significant increase in extreme poverty and undernourishment is observable in EBEN, with the majority of people not benefit ing from the minimum wage due to their informal employment or dependence on subsistence agriculture;  Conflic ts of interest: President NEEBOBY status as a major businessperson potentially conflicts with his commitment to advance economic transformati on.  Terror Groups: The country faces is affected by a complex crisis and instability coming from the cross -border r egion of the Liptako Gourma with TGs influx in the northern border areas from neighbouring FANBO and REGIN. Both the government of EBEN and external donors are considering preventive measures to avoid the spread of violence in the country.  Smuggling and tr afficking : EBEN faces a range of criminal market challenges, including human trafficking, arms trafficking, environmental crimes, and drug trafficking. Socio -cultural and ethnic similarities among border communities, mainly the Bayougi and the Felingu é, facilitate cooperation in illicit cross -border activities, supporting markets for smuggled

: EBEN faces a range of criminal market challenges, including human trafficking, arms trafficking, environmental crimes, and drug trafficking. Socio -cultural and ethnic similarities among border communities, mainly the Bayougi and the Felingu é, facilitate cooperation in illicit cross -border activities, supporting markets for smuggled fuel, drugs and weapons as well as environmental crimes. For all those, EBEN primarily serves as a transit country rather than a consumption market. Human trafficki ng involves primarily women and children from low -income families who are exploited in forced labour or sex trafficking in GABON, IGAINER, and the DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF THE CONGO (DRC). Foreign actors have a significant presence in the criminal markets of EBEN , with groups from both the DRC and from DUSHMAN operat ing in EBEN.  Maritime crime : The IMB Piracy Reporting Centre4 recorded a significant increase in attempted and successful attacks in EBEN’s waters over the last years. Most attacks involved attempts at kidnapping crews in exchange for ransom. With the rise of maritime crime in the Gulf of GUINEA , the region is the location of the vast majority of reported kidnappings at sea worldwide .  Social volatility: Despite its relatively stable democrati c tradition, EBEN has experienced episodes of social volatility. High levels of youth unemployment and poverty, coupled with inadequate access to education and healthcare, contribute to discontent among the population, particula rly in the remote areas. Political tensions, occasional protests, and labour strikes have arisen, reflecting public dissatisfaction. The regional division betwe en the north and the south as well as the struggle between nomadic Filengue herders and sedentary ethnic groups ov er access to grazing lands and water, land tenure, and crop damage and access to water have also led to localized confrontations. Moreover, the vulnerability of EBEN to external

the south as well as the struggle between nomadic Filengue herders and sedentary ethnic groups ov er access to grazing lands and water, land tenure, and crop damage and access to water have also led to localized confrontations. Moreover, the vulnerability of EBEN to external shocks, such as climate c hange, desertification and economic fluctuations in n eighbouring IGAINER bring further social volatility risks. 4: International Maritime Bureau NATO UNCLASSIFIED  Electricity: Only 29% of EBEN’s households have access to electricity. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Climate Short description: Tropical, hot, humid in south and semiarid in north. EBEN's climate is hot and humid and divide s into two climatic zones : the north and the south. Annual rainfall in the southern coastal area averages 1,360 mm, which is not particularly high for coastal West Africa. Coastal EBEN has two rainy and two dry seasons. The principal rainy season is from April to late July, with a shorter, less intense rainy period from late September to November. The main dry season is from December to April, with a short cooler dry season from late July to early September. Temperatures and humidity are high along the tropical coast. In Cotonou, th e average maximum temperature is 31 °C, while the minimum is 24 °C. Variations in temperature increase when moving north, through the savannah and plateau towards the Sahel. A dry wind from the Sahara, called the Harmattan, blows towards the south and west from December to March. Grass and vegetation turn dry with a veil of fine dust hanging over the country, causing the skies to be overcast. Farmers burn brush in the fields during this season . Precipitation is the key climatic variable, with most areas exp eriencing a clear alternation between wet and dry seasons. The length of the rainy season generally decreases moving northwards, with fewer rainy days and total annual precipitation. Political and Key Actor Landscape NATO UNCLASSIFIED Political system In the 1990's, The Republic of EBEN (EBEN) transitioned to a multiparty democracy with the adoption of a new constitution tha t guarantees human rights and political freedoms. The Republic of EBEN is a presidential republic with a three -tier system of gove rnment with an executive branch, a legislative branch, and a judicial branch. Traditional chieftaincy, which remains relevant, is constitutionally recognised as a guardian of habits and customs. Discussi ons regarding its legal framework are ongoing. Moreov er, especially across the border with IGAINER, traditional leaders have maintained an informal system of agreements to assist each other with respect to various social and cultural issues Political power The Tehoue, the Bayougi and the Owochi alongside other ethnic groups in the north such as the Harou and Felingu é in northern EBEN, constitute the centres of social and political power in EBEN. These groups constitute the three pre -colonial ancient kingdoms of south -centre Dahomey, south -eastern Hogbonou and northern Nikki respectively. Centred on the Owochi , these northern ethnic groups remain political significant in their struggle for national political power. Executive branch Executive power is

Executive branch Executive power is vested in the President as the Chief of State and the Head of the Government. He/she appoints, after an advisory opinion of the National Assembly, the members of the Government who remain answerable to the President. The President is elected by direct universal suffrage. The presidential mandate is five years , with a maximum of two consecutive presidential mandates. The President presides over the Council of Ministers. This Council deliberates and decides on general policies of the State, Government bills, ordnances and decrees. The President also signs laws passed to him/her by the National Assembly. The President, as the Supreme Commander of the Armed forces, is responsible for National Defence. He/she presides the Nationa l Council on Defence and Security. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Legislative branch The primary legislative authority is exercised through a unicameral National Assembly . The National Assembly has 109 members, elected by direct universal suffrage with an election cycle of four years. Members of the National Assembly can be re -elected. Members of the National Assembly have the law -making initiative through initiating draft bills on a wide range of topics. They are also responsible for approving the national budget and overseeing the activities of the executive branch. Parliamentary overs ight is conducted through several mechanisms of which the Parliamentary committee, examining government policies, programmes, and activities is the most relevant one. These committees have the power to summon the President and other member s of the Governme nt to answer questions and provide information. The National Assembly also provides oversight over the

The National Assembly also provides oversight over the budget allocation proc ess, approving or rejecting the national budget proposed by the Government. The National Assembly has the power to initiate impe achment procedure against the President, which are then led by the High Court of Justice. Despite the legislative power being an important source of checks and balances over the executive branch, the National Assemb ly has often been ineffective in providi ng this function. Moreover, corruption remains a substantial problem, and many National Assembly members have been accused of using their position for personal gain, especially those with significant business interests in the commercially developed souther n hub of Cotonou. Judicial branch The legal system is strongly influenced by the French colonial period and combines modern written law and customary law under pinned by traditional customary rules. Despite attempts to codify particular provisions such as personal and family laws, customary law remains the main instrument for legal issues over land tenure. By virtue of the Constitution, the court system in EBEN is established as follows. At the highest level, there is the Supreme Court and the High Court of Justice. The Supreme Court is the highest court and all decisions from the court are binding for the executive power, the legislative power, as well as for all courts of law. The High Court of Justice is a special court competent to judge the Pres ident and other members of Government for charges of high treason and/or infractions committed in the exercise of their duties. Challenges While EBEN's political system is structured around democratic principles, recent developments indicate significant challenges to its democratic integrity and institutional independence. Despite its initial reputation as a beacon of democracy in West Africa, EBEN faces challenges such as:  Erosion of judicial independence.  Restrictions o n press freedom and civil liberties.  Increased political repression against opposition parties. Since 2016, there have been concerns regarding democratic backsliding under the President’s administration. Electoral reforms introduced in 2018 have been criticized for restricting opposition participation, leading to a parliament dominated by the President's supporters. Governance and administration at the sub -national level Governance is organised around a hierarchical structure that is comprised of departments , communes, and local councils. EBEN counts twelve departments, each further divided into communes, which are the second -tier administrative units. The communes are subdivided into arrondissements , providing a more localized level of administrat ion. At the lowest level, local councils represent the smallest administrative entities and are responsible for local governance and decision -making. This structure reflects a largely decentralised and devolved system, enabling more autonomy for local comm unities. Political parties and interest groups Political parties have since the 1990s presented themselves either independently or as part of often -changing political alliances. The political landscape i s largely fragmented by a large number of parties , many of which are very small . The majority of the parties in EBEN lack internal democratic rules and transparency. These are often considered to be the private property of the founder or financier and to p redominantly serve the latter or his family’s interests. Most are anchored in one specific department and/or within one specific ethnic group or clan/tribe. A limited number of political parties (see below) have been able to tr anscend local or ethnic interests. Due to the shifting nature of political alliances between the parties, political ideologies have become increasingly fluid. T he most prominent form of political distinctio n remains the cleavage between the north and south. Militant groups (Community protection groups) EBEN, as with other countri es in the Sahel region , has a history of community -based self -defence entities. These groups are deeply rooted in their territories. During political rebellions across the Sahel region in the 1980s and 1990s, a second phase in these groups’ existence emerg ed. This partly stemmed from the state's desire to support and rely on these groups to counter rebel groups. Gradually however, the initial security -related role of these groups evolved: They have been used to protect political networks and have also sough t their own enrichment, especially in gold mining areas. The employment of these groups as part of CT and counter -insurgency efforts has however not always improved security. Allegations have been made of self - defence groups engaging in illegal militia -like activities, sometimes in collaboration with the national defence and security forces , such as kidnappings, property destruction, sexual abuse, involvement in drug trafficking and the recruitment of children . This has served to increase pressure on displaced populations. Moreover, in northern EBEN, the Felingué community is particularly vulnerable to violence from these groups. NATO UNCLASSIFIED Border security forces The Border Police Unit (BPU) , falling under the National Police, is the primary agency dedicated to border security and border management. The BPU is responsible for border surveillance and preventing cross -border crimes such as human trafficking, drug trafficking, arms smuggling, and irregular migration. The BPU is the first responder in the event of a border incident given that it collaborates with law enforcement agencies in neig hbouring coun tries and international partners. The Directorate for Emigration and Immigration (Direction de l’Émigration et de l’Immigration (DIE)) is an integral part of the National Police which provides passport and visa control at border crossings. The Agency for the Integrated Management of Border Spaces further augments control of border areas where cross -border ethnic and family ties render proper border management and control difficult. This Agency improves living conditions, bringing central government and loc al communities together through infrastructure development, poverty reduction, border security (as an intelligence source to the EBEN Armed Forces) and cross -border cooperation. The Customs and Excise Department (Direction Générale des Douanes et Droits In directs) is responsible for overseeing trade, taxation, and customs -related matters, regulating and monitoring goods and people crossing the borders. The Government’s grip on the parks through the APN to develop touri sm clashes with the interests of both hunters, pastoralists, local farmers and villagers who have been denied access to th e parks. French colonial rule and subsequent close ties with France have left a deep impact on all aspects of cultural life, especially among the educated segments of the population and in the southern cities. Each ethnic group also has its own centuries -old tradition, which itself often mixes with the French influence. These cultural traditions are clustered in two distinct regions, the largely Muslim north an d the largely animist and Ch ristian south. This has an impact on how and where the population receives their information. Religious leaders and community leaders theref ore have more sway and influence in the rural north. Independence and freedom of the media . In reality pr ess freedom has suffered major setbacks over recent years. The government exercises a decisive influence over the appointment of those in charge of the state -owned media and the High Authority for Broadcasting and Communication (HAAC), the media regulator. The state -run Office de Radio diffusion et de Television du EBEN ( ORTN) , especially its television channels, is required to relay the government’s message, while media that support the opposition are under strong pressure. Many media outlets refrain from openly criticising the government in order to avoid a shutdown by the HAAC. Freedom of expression : In theory, journalists and media outlets are free to cover all matters of public interest, but in fact, the government has, in recent years, strengthened its grip on the sector. Since 2016, journalists have had limited access to data relating to the country ’s security situation, internally displaced persons, and mining contracts. A pro -opposition radio station was forced to stop broadcasting in 2019 during elections. The deterioration of the security situation in the north of the country has resulted in repeated press freedom violations, including the detention and deportation of journalists. Fight against disinformation . Disinformation has emerged as a significant challenge in EBEN, particularly in the context of political dynamics and the increasing use of digital media. The government and civil society organizations are actively working to combat the spread of false infor mation. In recent years, EBEN has seen an increase in laws aimed at regulating false information. However, these laws have been criticized for potentially being used to suppress dissent rather than effectively combat misinformation. Diplomacy Placing economic diplomacy at the heart of its foreign policy has generated economic development benefits for EBEN. Alignment of EBEN with USA positions has proven beneficial, facilitating military cooperation and the provision of development aid. As a consequence of this, EBEN has been more resistant to recent MURINUS political overtures than some countries in the region. At the same time, EBEN has been open to DUSHMAN business prop ositions, though some senior figures in the regime are becoming more aware of the need for caution in giving DUSHMAN wide -ranging influence in EBEN commercial affairs. The engagement of EBEN’s modest military instrument s of power as part of regional collective security and the participation of EBEN in various peacekeeping operations has also been applied as means of securing diplomatic dividends. Its regional diplomatic perspectives are as follows: IGAINER Relations with IGAINER have been generally positive and stable. There ha ve been no t erritorial border dispute s between IGAINER and EBEN , though border crossing points have on several occasions been temporary closed for various reasons: such as to curb illicit cross -border trade or smuggling of narcotics , or else of small arms and light weapons. Additionally, there have been issues concerning irregular migration and human trafficking. Despite these tensions and challenges, both countries have a positive track record of diplomatic engagement and cooperation in order to tackle such issues. NATO UNCLASSIFIED FANBO There has been one territorial border dispute between FANBO and EBEN over a 68 km2 area of Kouou ( also named Koalu ) near the border triangle with GOOT. According to an agreement during French colonial rule, the area belonged to FANBO , but this was di sputed by EBEN. After tensions rose in the early 2000s, a 2009 agreement declared the area a neutral zone. Due to the delicate situation of the area, there is a lack of security as both FANBO and EBEN forces deserted the area in ord er to avoid a diplomati c incident. That is why Koalou/Kourou became an epicentre of an illicit cross -border fuel trade and also allowed for TGs to operate gold mines in the area and tax smugglers. The problem has been reduced following a crackdown on the area by EBEN troops in 2 023. However, it did not come without controversy from FANBO. REGIN There has been one territorial border dispute between REGIN and EBEN over their border along the Niger River and the status o f the island of Lété Goungou in the rive r. EBEN claimed the island, but the International Court of Justice in 2005 determined the course of the Niger River as the boundary between the two States . On this basis, the court ruled that Lété Goungou belonged to REGIN.
